titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John Farris Papers MSS.632
titlestmt.author: Arranged into series and described by Stacey Flatt, and in December 2023 was stabilized and described by Rachel Searcy

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-05-15 12:37:06 UTC.

physdesc.extent: in 12 manuscript boxes, 2 small flat boxes, 3 oversize flat boxes, 1 object in shared housing, and 12 flat-file folders

did.abstract: John Farris (1940–2016) was a writer and artist who lived in New York's East Village and was active in the Downtown literary and jazz scenes from the 1970s until his death in 2016. A prolific poet and visual artist, Farris performed frequently but published relatively little during his lifetime. He was closely associated with the artistic collective Bullet Space and the organization A Gathering of the Tribes. His life and work reflect the vibrant artistic communities of Black musicians, artists, and writers in the late 20th century in New York City. The John Farris Papers (1970s–2020) document Farris' artistic and personal life. The collection includes drafts and manuscripts of his poetry, books, short stories, and plays, with significant documentation of his major works, including The Ass's Tale (2010) and It's Not About Time (1994). Promotional materials, and digital and analog recordings of readings and events held in New York City from the late 1970s through the 2010s are also present in this collection. Farris's reviews of local music, art, and books, along with transcripts of interviews with artists, writers, and musicians are also included. The collection further contains a large selection of drawings and sketches and a small amount of exhibition promotional material. Documentation of his personal life includes correspondence, photographs, an oral history recording, and his files related to the Bullet Space Co-op Tenant Association where he was a resident.
did.physloc: Materials are stored offsite and advance notice is required for use. Please request materials at least two business days prior to your research visit to coordinate access.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Andrew Castrucci on behalf of the John Farris Estate in July 2023; the accession number associated with this purchase is 2023.064.

appraisal.head: Appraisal
appraisal.p: In 2023, one laptop was appraised out of this collection. In 2026 the following items were removed from the collection during processing: 11 born-digital carriers (commercial and duplicate material), and one and a half manuscript boxes containing duplicate copies of writings, print material, and blurry photographs.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p:
Organized into 3 series, with Series I. divided further into 5 subseries. Further series and subseries arrangement notes are described on that level:
Series I. Writings
Subseries I.A. Poems
Subseries I.B. Books
Subseries I.C. Short Stories and Plays
Subseries I.D. Non-Fiction Writing and Editing
Subseries I.E. Events and Performances
Series II. Artworks
Series III. Personal

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: John Farris (1940–2016) was a poet, novelist, and visual artist who lived in New York's East Village and was active in the Downtown literary and jazz scenes. Although a prolific writer and performer, he published relatively little during his lifetime. Farris was closely associated with the artistic collective Bullet Space, the literary collective The Unbearables, and A Gathering of the Tribes. His published works include the novel The Ass's Tale, It's Not About Time, and Last Poems (2025), published posthumously. In 2008, he was named Poet Laureate of the Lower East Side by the Howl! Festival, and in 2013 he received an Acker Award for his lifetime contributions to the arts.

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: An item-level list of artworks for each portfolio treated by the NYU Preservation Lab in 2023 (portfolios #9-15) is available in the curatorial file for this collection. This list contains preservation lab staff-assigned titles for the artworks, format, and dimensions for each work.

phystech.head: Audiovisual Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Audiovisual materials have not been preserved and may not be available to researchers. Materials not yet digitized will need to have access copies made before they can be used. To request an access copy, or if you are unsure if an item has been digitized, please contact Fales Library and Special Collections, special.collections@nyu.edu, 212-998-2596 with the collection name, collection number, and a description of the item(s) requested. A staff member will respond to you with further information.

phystech.head: Born-Digital Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Advance notice is required for the use of computer records. Original physical digital media is restricted. An access terminal for born-digital materials in the collection is available by appointment for reading room viewing and listening only. Researchers may view an item's original container and/or carrier, but the physical carriers themselves are not available for use because of preservation concerns.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date; John Farris Papers; MSS 632; box number; folder number or item identifier; Fales Library and Special Collections, New York University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p:
At the time of accessioning materials were rehoused in archival boxes and folders in their original order. Materials were described on the collection-level with a box-level inventory.
Drawings were organized by size and grouped into appropriate folder sizes. Drawings with friable media (charcoal or heavily applied graphite) were interleaved with glassine. Small drawings were placed on top of larger drawings. Drawings were surface cleaned with pre-soaked non-latex cosmetic sponges. Crumpled drawings were humidified and flattened between blotters. Tears were mended with wheat starch paste and kozo fiber Japanese paper.
In 2026, paper material was placed in acid-free folders and manuscript boxes. Oversized material was foldered and housed appropriately according to its size. Press clippings, publications, and journals were sorted; 1 copy retained. New York Times articles were removed from the collection. Writings were sorted by format (poems, books, plays/short stories, articles/interviews/reviews) and organized in alphabetical order by title.
A large portion of the artworks were originally stored in plastic portfolios and rehoused by Preservation Lab staff during accessioning in 2023. Although these works have been physically separated into appropriately sized folders and boxes, they are intellectually organized according to their original portfolio groupings. The processing archivist assigned each portfolio grouping a number and recorded the total number of items within each grouping in the title (e.g., "Portfolio 1 – 65 items"). Folders containing artworks from each portfolio are listed under the corresponding grouping.
An edited version of the item-level spreadsheet provided by the Preservation Lab was downloaded into the curatorial collection folder.
A select number of artworks had remained in plastic portfolios and were not treated by the Preservation Lab. These portfolios were disassembled and artworks were removed, rehoused into folders, and organized according to their assigned "portfolio number." These groupings are described and listed in the inventory following the same structure used for previously treated materials.
Born-digital materials on physical carriers were identified, physically separated, inventoried, and forensically imaged. New York University Libraries follow professional standards and best practices when imaging, ingesting, and processing born-digital material in order to maintain the integrity and authenticity of the content. 18 born-digital carriers were forensically imaged and analyzed. The 7 carriers retained in the collection were arranged and described.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
The John Farris Papers (1970s–2020) comprise materials created and assembled by Farris that document both his artistic work and personal life. The collection includes writings in various stages of development, artworks, and personal ephemera.
Farris's writing is particularly well represented, encompassing both creative and practical output. Materials include drafts and manuscripts of his poetry, books, short stories, and plays, in various stages of completion. Poetry constitutes a substantial portion of the collection, documenting individual poems through multiple revisions as well as in finished form, and illustrating his creative process. Notable works include manuscripts and drafts of his book The Ass's Tale (2010) and his 1994 volume of poetry It's Not About Time. The collection also contains documentation of his editorial work, including materials related to a volume of poetry by Joseph Semenovich.
Also included are articles, reviews, and interviews by Farris. His reviews, focused on music, art, and film, document cultural activity in the Downtown New York City area from the 1990s through the 2010s. Interviews with artists, writers, poets, and musicians were often developed into articles for local publications; related files typically include handwritten notes, background research, and typed transcripts. Promotional materials, along with digital and analog recordings of readings and events held at various New York venues date from the late 1970s to 2011.
Farris's visual artwork is also represented in this collection through drawings and sketches, including portraits, group scenes, landscapes and cityscapes, and cartoons with often political or satirical captions. A small number of exhibition fliers and posters are also present.
Materials relating to his personal life include correspondence, photographs, an audio recording of an oral history interview, and documentation of his involvement with the Bullet Space Co-op Tenant Association. His obituary, memorial service program, and accompanying images are also in this collection, dated from 2016.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: This collection is protected by copyright and/or related rights. You are free to use materials in the collection in any way that is permitted by the copyright and related rights legislation that applies to your use. For other uses you need to obtain permission from the rights-holder(s).

controlaccess.subject: Poets, American.
Novelists, American.
Beats (Persons)
African American poets
Short stories, American -- 20th century.
Artists and community -- New York (N.Y)
Artists -- Dwellings -- New York (State) -- New York.

physdesc.extent: in 10 manuscript boxes, 1 oversize flat box, and 1 item in a shared box

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p:
This series is divided into five subseries:
Subseries I.A. Poems
Subseries I.B. Books
Subseries I.C. Short Stories and Plays
Subseries I.D. Non-Fiction Writing and Editing
Subseries I.E. Events and Performances

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Farris's writing is well represented in this series, encompassing both his creative and practical output. The series includes drafts and manuscripts of his poetry, books, short stories, and plays, in various stages of completion. Documentation of his editorial work is also present, along with promotional materials and audio and video recordings from readings and other events.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is arranged alphabetically by poem title. Within each alphabetical section, a folder labeled by letter contains poems for which only a single copy exists

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: A creator-supplied title containing racist language was identified in this subseries, but has been retained to convey important contextual information regarding the artists choice of language for titles.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains poetry written by Farris, including both handwritten and typed copies. The majority of the files document individual poems in various stages of revision, as well as finished versions. Together, these materials illustrate his creative process as a poet.

did.unittitle: "Another Day, Another Spring (at soon to be 67)"
"Feeding", in A Gathering of the Tribes
"Notebook of Poems Dedicated to Daughter Jade"
"Joey in Short Pants \ A Rare Picture of Joseph Semenovich"

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The title of this poem is supplied by the creator and has been retained to convey important contextual information regarding the poet's choice of language.

did.unittitle: "On Inclusion in the Outlaw Bible of American Poetry"
"The Queen of the Bowery Lives in a Tin Palace"
"Quietly, Classically, The Company Has Us Sur"

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is arranged chronologically, with files grouped by book title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains manuscripts and drafts for both published and unpublished books by Farris. It includes mock-ups and proofs, as well as a video recording of the book launch for his 1994 volume of poetry It's Not About Time. A significant number of paper and digital drafts and manuscripts for the 2010 book The Ass's Tale dating back to 1995 are also present. The subseries further includes multiple versions of the manuscript Ubiquitous Grain of Salt, represented by handwritten and typed drafts under varying titles.

did.unittitle: Mock-up Proofs and Event Promotional Material
Recording of Book Party with BADU
The Ass's Tale (2010) -- Unedited Version of First Draft
The Ass's Tale (2010) -- Typed, Edited Manuscripts
The Ass's Tale (2010) -- Correspondence, Synopsis, and Cover Photograph
Ubiquitous Grain of Salt -- Handwritten and Typed Drafts
Unpublishable -- Published Anthology with Farris Bio and Works
Subseries I.C. Short Stories and Plays

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is grouped by subject and alphabetically within by title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains drafts and manuscripts of short stories and play scripts by Farris, including a manuscript, program and performance recording of the short story "Terra Cotta Pigeon Clay Crow," as well as a script and performance program for The Patient.

did.unittitle: Short Story -- Short Stories with Character, George
Short Story -- "If You Leave You Lose Your Sent"
Short Story -- "Nostalgia in One Hundred & Twenty Fifth Street
Short Story -- "Terra Cotta Pigeon Clay Crow"
Play -- & When the Wagon Comes, Etcetera
Play -- In the Park After School with the Girl & The Boy
Subseries I.D. Non-Fiction Writing and Editing

physdesc.extent: in 2 manuscript boxes and 1 oversize flat box

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is grouped alphabetically by subject.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
This subseries contains articles, reviews, interviews, and related projects by Farris. The reviews focused on music, art, and film, which documents the cultural activities in Downtown New York City from the 1990s through the 2010s.
Interviews conducted by Farris, often with artists, writers, poets, and musicians were typically developed into articles for local publications. Files on individual interviewees in this subseries generally include handwritten notes, background research on the subject or musical group, and typed transcripts. Together, these materials document both the creative work of these individuals and the communities in which they were active, offering a snapshot of the cultural landscape of the period. Farris also worked as an editor for various writers, and this subseries contains a selection of his editorial files, including materials related to a volume of poetry by Joseph Semenovich.

did.unittitle: Articles -- Art, Jazz, Writers, Bullet Space History
Interview -- Ersahini, Ilhan: The Nublu Concept
Interview -- Jackson, Hyana V. and Marco Villalobos
Interview -- Johnston, Aaron and Jesse Murphy

physdesc.extent: in 2 manuscript boxes and 1 item in a shared box

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains paper materials, and digital and analog recordings related to Farris' readings and events. Recordings of programs featuring readings of poetry and short stories, which were often combined with music date from the 1990s through the 2010s, while promotional fliers and mailers in the form of paper and digital files span the late 1970s to 2011. These events took place at various New York venues and clubs, including the Living Theater, Bullet Space, and ArtLoft Gallery. Of particular note is Farris's 1977 Children's Art Carnival in which he worked with children to encourage them to write poetry. Materials related to this project include a press clipping, promotional fliers, and examples of poetry.

did.unittitle: Children's Art Carnival -- Press Clipping, Promotional Fliers, and Children's Poetry
Nubila Latrare, "The Set Up" Nuyorican at the Living Theatre
Earth Bird Presents The Poetry of John Farris
Closing Party at The Bullet Space Events with Farris Reading
City Birds with Poet John Farris, Chris Hofman, & Cello at ArtLoft Gallery
City Birds with Poet John Farris, Chris Hofman, & Cello at ArtLoft Gallery
Audio Recording of Unidentified Reading and Music Event

daodesc.p: Audio Recording of Unidentified Reading and Music Event

did.unittitle: John Farris Video by Ari Marcopoulos at the Living Theatre
WFMU Broadcast "The Black Ass Of..." (Excerpt) -- John Farris, Nina Simone "Papa Can You Hear Me", "Dial Africa" John Coltrane

physdesc.extent: in 1 manuscript box, 2 small flat boxes, 3 oversize flat box, and 12 flat-file folders

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged beginning with chronological files, followed by archivist-assigned numbered portfolios and oversize artwork in flat-files which were removed from the portfolios but were not notated with their portfolio numbers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
This series contains physical drawings and sketches by John Farris. The majority of the works are undated sketches in pencil, charcoal, or pen, with subjects which include self-portraits, portraits of friends and family, group scenes, landscapes and cityscapes, and cartoons with captions that often reflect a political or satirical perspective. A smaller number of artworks are executed in watercolor.
The series also includes a limited number of promotional fliers and posters for exhibitions of Farris' works. It also includes a small collection of digital images of selected artworks, likely created for exhibition purposes.

did.unittitle: Exhibition and Related Event Announcements and Ephemera

physdesc.extent: in 4 manuscript boxes and 1 small flat box

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged alphabetically by subject.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
Materials in this series relate to John Farris's personal life and include correspondence, photographs, and other ephemera. His correspondence documents communication with family and friends, as well as general incoming letters. Digital and print photographs of friends and family are also included in this series, as well as his resume and business card. Materials related to his death, such as his obituary, memorial service program, and accompanying images date from 2016.
An undated audio recording of an oral history interview with Farris is in this series, along with documentary film dailies from 2002 containing footage of him in his apartment. Documentation of his involvement with the Bullet Space Co-op Tenant Association dates from 2002 to 2014, reflecting efforts to establish and maintain a living space within his building and artistic community.

did.unittitle: Correspondence -- Sheppard, Sundance Etienne Latour Genore Hughes
Housing Documentation -- Bullet Space Co-op Tenant Association
Photographs, Contact Sheets, and Negatives -- Farris Portraits, Musicians, Concerts, Readings
Photographs -- Farris Portraits, and Farris with Phoebe and Child
Photographs -- Farris and Friends, and Events
